narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 74% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, well below the typical 5.7; a Year 3 decile of 1, even lower than the average 5.5; an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly under the usual 3.21; a low LSAT score of 25.5 compared to the standard 36.7; and a lower-middle family income bracket, less than the dataset's average of 3.47—while your status as a white male pursuing full-time studies aligns closely with the majority of students (over 80% white, 56% male, and 93% full-time). The model's decision was driven primarily by your LSAT score, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, then your Year 1 decile, family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status. Each of these factors nudged the prediction higher toward bar failure risk, with none providing a strong counterbalance toward success. For instance, your notably low LSAT and school deciles stood out as bigger drags compared to typical applicants, while your slightly below-average GPA and income added to the concern. In contrast, most admitted students have LSATs around 37, mid-range deciles near 5-6, and GPAs over 3.2, tipping their profiles toward passing predictions. This ranking shows your standardized test and early academic performance in school were the top hurdles, outweighing your more average demographics. To improve your odds next time, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that critical score—many students gain 5-10 points with targeted prep—and focus intensely on excelling in your coursework from Year 1 onward to climb those school deciles, as these are areas within your control through dedication and study strategies. Your undergraduate GPA might still be shined up with strong law school performance, though family background is harder to shift. With these changes, you could realign your profile closer to successful students and flip the prediction in your favor—keep pushing, as many have turned similar starts around.
